---
title: "Research"
---

My research is located at the intersection of health economics, behavioural economics, mathematical psychology, and public policy. I use stated-preference methods, especially discrete choice experiments, to study how people evaluate health technologies, public health policies, and healthcare delivery models.

## Main themes

### Preference heterogeneity and choice modelling

I study how preferences differ across individuals and groups, and how heterogeneity affects inference, willingness-to-pay estimates, predicted uptake, and policy interpretation.

### Information processing and attention

A central strand of my work examines how respondents process complex choice information. This includes research on cost information, eye tracking, numeracy, feedback, and dominance-structured tasks.

### Public health policy and mandates

I examine public preferences for vaccine mandates and other public health interventions, with a focus on legitimacy, proportionality, exemption design, and policy thresholds.

### Equity and distributional preferences

My work also studies how people trade off self-interest, population benefit, and concern for worse-off groups in healthcare and public policy decisions.

## Methods

- Discrete choice experiments
- Mixed logit and latent class models
- Best–worst and dual-response choice tasks
- Eye-tracking and process-tracing methods
- Experimental survey design
- Health preference and decision-quality assessment
